name: Achelous
description: Achelous is the Greek god of the Achelous River, the largest river in Greece. He is the son of Oceanus and Tethys, making him one of the Oceanids. Achelous possessed the power to change his form at will, a common trait among river gods. He was known for his strength and virility, and was often depicted as a bull-man, reflecting the river's powerful and sometimes destructive nature. Achelous played a significant role in several myths, most notably his struggle with Heracles for the hand of Deianira. During this contest, he transformed into various forms, including a serpent and a bull, but was ultimately defeated by Heracles, who broke off one of his horns. This horn was then transformed into the Cornucopia, the horn of plenty, by the nymphs. Achelous represents the untamed power of rivers, their ability to both nourish and destroy, and the constant struggle between nature and civilization. He is a testament to the ancient Greeks' deep connection to the natural world and their personification of its forces. He is also associated with oaths and boundaries, as rivers often served as natural borders. His defeat by Heracles is interpreted as the triumph of human ingenuity and strength over the raw power of nature.
subtitle: God of the Achelous River

keyMyths.title: The Contest for Deianira
keyMyths.description: Achelous and Heracles fought for the hand of Deianira. Achelous transformed into a serpent and a bull during the battle, but Heracles ultimately prevailed, breaking off one of Achelous's horns.

keyMyths.title: The Origin of the Cornucopia
keyMyths.description: After Heracles broke off Achelous's horn, the nymphs transformed it into the Cornucopia, a symbol of abundance and plenty, forever associated with the river god's defeat and subsequent transformation.

keyMyths.title: Achelous and the Echinades Islands
keyMyths.description: According to legend, the Echinades Islands were formed from nymphs who neglected to honor Achelous in their rites. He flooded the land, creating the islands as punishment for their disrespect.
